---
title: "Final Exam Spring 2026"
subtitle: "MKT 378 — Marketing Research"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
---

```{r}
#| include: false
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)
```

## Overview and Instructions

Alright everyone, this is it — the final exam.

The exam and the unique dataset generator are all posted in the class final exam folder.

As was the case for the midterm, I expect that completing the exam will likely take **6–10 hours** depending on your comfort level with the material. I strongly suggest you do not wait until the due date to start.

This page is a **guide only**. It previews the structure of the exam, lists the models you will be asked to estimate, and summarizes the kinds of questions asked in each part. It does not correctly code variables for you, provide interpretations or numerical answers, or contain fully working code beyond specifying model templates.

### What you need to do

1. Download your unique dataset from the dataset generator app linked below, using your **Student ID as the seed value**.
2. Work through the exam questions, using the support material on this page (and/or in previous exercises!) as your guide.
3. Submit your answers in the **Final Exam Spring 2026 quiz** on Brightspace. 

As a reminder, along with your answers you will need to submit:
   - Your Student ID (seed value)
   - Your adapted R code (top-left panel of RStudio)
   - The console output generated by running your code (bottom-left panel of RStudio)
   - Any and all AI use disclosures consistent with class policy

---

## Dataset Generator

Use the link below to generate your unique dataset. **You must use your own Student ID as the seed value.** If you do not use your Student ID, I will not be able to reproduce your dataset for grading and you will not receive credit. The filename of your downloaded dataset will automatically include your seed value, which makes it easy to verify.

> **[https://erinpcarter.shinyapps.io/FinalExamDataGenerator/]**

---

## The Scenario

You have just joined the analytics team at **Evergreen Subscriptions**, a mid-sized direct-to-consumer company that sells curated monthly product boxes. Your first assignment comes directly from the Chief Marketing Officer, who wants to understand what drives monthly customer spending and whether the company's current email and promotional strategies are actually working.

The company has been operating on a set of standing assumptions about their customer base:

| Variable | Company Assumption |
|---|---|
| Average monthly spend | $80.00 |
| Typical emails sent per month | 4 |
| Promotion effectiveness | Flat $10 lift in spending |
| Engagement effect | Linear and uniform across all customers |

Your job is to put these assumptions to the test using a sample of 1,500 customer records. Along the way, you will build increasingly sophisticated models — adding engagement, income, promotion interactions, nonlinear effects, and tenure — to understand not just whether these drivers matter, but *how* they matter and *for whom*.

Your CMO is analytically curious but not a statistician. Throughout the exam, she will ask questions like:

- "Does sending more emails actually move the needle, or are we just assuming it does?"
- "Does our promo work the same way for every customer?"
- "Is there a point of diminishing returns on engagement or is more always better?"
- "Should we be treating all customers the same, or are there meaningful segments we're missing?"

Your answers need to be based on the data and your analyses.

---

## Data Dictionary

| Variable | Description | Type |
|----------|-------------|------|
| `Student_ID` | Your seed value | Numeric |
| `Customer_ID` | Anonymous customer record ID | Numeric |
| `Monthly_Spend` | Total dollars spent by the customer last month | Numeric (dollars) |
| `Emails_Per_Month` | Number of promotional emails sent to the customer last month | Numeric (count) |
| `Web_Engagement` | Customer engagement on a 1–5 scale (higher = more engaged) | Numeric (1–5) |
| `Promo` | Promotion status: 0 = no promotion, 1 = received a promotion | Binary (0/1) |
| `Age` | Customer age in years (18 or older) | Numeric (years) |
| `Tenure_Months` | Months the customer has been subscribed | Numeric (months) |
| `Credit_Score` | Approximate credit score (500–850) | Numeric |
| `Personal_Salary` | Estimated personal annual salary in dollars | Numeric (dollars) |
| `HH_Income_Pred` | Predicted total household income in dollars | Numeric (dollars) |

---

## A Note on Fitting Models Before Answering Questions

Each section of the exam specifies the model or models you need to fit before answering the questions in that section. Make sure you have actually run the relevant `lm()` call and have the output in front of you before submitting answers. The exam questions will ask you to report coefficients, significance, predictions, and PRE values — all of which come directly from your R output.

---

## Support Code

The sections below mirror the structure of the exam. You can work through them section by section prior to opening the exam.

---

### Setup

Load the required package and your dataset. Replace the filename with the name of the file you downloaded from the generator.

```r
library(dplyr)

exam2 <- read.csv("mkt378_exam2_data_YOURID.csv")

head(exam2)
str(exam2)
```

---

### Part 1 — Centering and Foundations (Questions 1–6)

**Q1** — You will enter your Student ID (the same value used as the seed in the data generator).

**Q2** (conceptual MC) — What does it mean to center a predictor such as `Emails_Per_Month`?

**Q3** (conceptual MC) — Why is centering or recoding predictors especially useful in models with interactions and/or quadratic terms?

**Q4–Q6** (text entry) — You will determine what value would be coded as 0 when you create each of the following centered variables: `emails_c` from `Emails_Per_Month`, `engage_c` from `Web_Engagement`, and `tenure_c` from `Tenure_Months`. These are dataset-specific values.

---

### Part 2 — Simple Regression (Questions 7–14)

You will create a centered email variable (`emails_c`) and fit the following models.

**Model_1:** `Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c`

```r
Model_1 <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c, data = exam2)
summary(Model_1)
```

**Q7** (text entry) — Report the slope for `emails_c` from Model_1.

**Q8** (MC) — Is the effect of `emails_c` statistically significant at α = .05?

**Q9** (MC) — Interpret the slope of `emails_c` in business terms.

**Q10** (MC) — Reflect on what it means to center `Emails_Per_Month` at a non-integer mean and then interpret the intercept in that model.

**Q11** (text entry) — Recode the email variable so that 0 represents the most frequently observed (most typical) value of `Emails_Per_Month` (call this `emails_typical`). Fit Model_1B and report the intercept.

**Model_1B:** `Monthly_Spend ~ emails_typical`

```r
Model_1B <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ emails_typical, data = exam2)
summary(Model_1B)
```

**Model_1C:** `Monthly_Spend ~ Emails_Per_Month`

```r
Model_1C <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ Emails_Per_Month, data = exam2)
summary(Model_1C)
```

**Q12** (text entry) — Using Model_1C, report the predicted `Monthly_Spend` when `Emails_Per_Month` = 0.

**Q13** (text entry) — Using Model_1C, report the predicted `Monthly_Spend` when `Emails_Per_Month` = 2.

**Q14** (MC) — What is the conceptual meaning of the difference between predicted spend at 0 and 2 emails?

---

### Part 3 — Multiple Regression (Questions 15–26)

You will create centered versions of the income variables (`hh_income_c` from `HH_Income_Pred` and `personal_salary_c` from `Personal_Salary`) and fit the following models.

**Model_2A:** `Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c + engage_c + Promo + hh_income_c`

```r
Model_2A <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c + engage_c + Promo + hh_income_c,
               data = exam2)
summary(Model_2A)
```

**Model_2B:** `Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c + engage_c + Promo + personal_salary_c`

```r
Model_2B <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c + engage_c + Promo + personal_salary_c,
               data = exam2)
summary(Model_2B)
```

**Q15–Q17** (text entry) — From Model_2A, report the slopes for `engage_c`, `emails_c`, and `hh_income_c`.

**Q18–Q20** (text entry) — From Model_2B, report the slopes for `engage_c`, `emails_c`, and `personal_salary_c`.

**Q21** (multi-select) — Which predictors are statistically significant at α = .05 in Model_2A?

**Q22** (multi-select) — Which predictors are statistically significant at α = .05 in Model_2B?

**Q23** (MC) — Comparing R² from Model_1 and Model_2A, which explains more variance in `Monthly_Spend`?

**Q24** (MC) — Comparing R² from Model_2A and Model_2B, which explains more variance?

**Q25** (MC) — Interpret the partial effect of `hh_income_c` in Model_2A.

**Q26** (short answer) — In 2–3 sentences, discuss what Model_2A and Model_2B suggest about behavioral drivers (engagement, emails) versus financial capacity (income). Which income measure was a better addition to the model?

---

### Part 4 — Two-Way Interaction: Emails × Promo (Questions 27–34)

**Model_3:** `Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c + Promo + emails_c*Promo`

```r
Model_3 <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c + Promo + emails_c*Promo,
              data = exam2)
summary(Model_3)
```

**Q27** (text entry) — Report the coefficient for the interaction term `emails_c:Promo`.

**Q28** (MC) — Is the interaction statistically significant at α = .05?

**Q29** (text entry) — Using Model_3 (where Promo = 0 means no promo), report the effect of an additional email on `Monthly_Spend` when `Promo` = 0.

**Q30** (text entry) — After recoding Promo so that 0 corresponds to receiving the promo, report the effect of an additional email on `Monthly_Spend` for customers who do receive the promo.

**Q31** (text entry) — Report the predicted `Monthly_Spend` for a customer who receives 5 emails and no promo.

**Q32** (MC) — How should you interpret a predicted value generated at `Emails_Per_Month` = 300 with a promo?

**Q33** (MC) — What is the main problem with predicting `Monthly_Spend` for someone who receives −5 emails and a promo?

**Q34** (MC) — Based on Model_3, how does promotion change the effectiveness of email volume?

---

### Part 5 — Nonlinear and Moderated Quadratic Effects (Questions 35–42)

`engage_c` is a mean-centered version of `Web_Engagement`. You will include a quadratic term and its interaction with `Promo`.

**Model_4A:** `Monthly_Spend ~ engage_c + I(engage_c^2) + Promo`

```r
Model_4A <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ engage_c + I(engage_c^2) + Promo,
               data = exam2)
summary(Model_4A)
```

**Model_4B:** `Monthly_Spend ~ engage_c + I(engage_c^2) + Promo + I(engage_c^2)*Promo`

```r
Model_4B <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ engage_c + I(engage_c^2) + Promo +
                               I(engage_c^2)*Promo,
               data = exam2)
summary(Model_4B)
```

**Q35** (text entry) — From Model_4A, report the coefficient for the quadratic term `I(engage_c^2)`.

**Q36** (multi-select) — In Model_4A, which engagement-related predictors are significant at α = .05?

**Q37** (text entry) — From Model_4B, report the coefficient for the interaction `I(engage_c^2):Promo`.

**Q38** (multi-select) — In Model_4B, which predictors are significant at α = .05?

**Q39** (MC) — Interpret a negative and significant quadratic term in Model_4A. What does that shape imply about the relationship between engagement and spending?

**Q40** (text entry) — Using Model_4B, compute the turning point (the level of `engage_c` that maximizes predicted `Monthly_Spend`) when there is no promo.

**Q41** (text entry) — Using Model_4B, compute the turning point when there is a promo.

**Q42** (MC) — What is the managerial implication of the turning point results from Model_4B?

---

### Part 6 — Three-Way Interaction: Emails × Promo × Tenure (Questions 43–50)

You will create a centered tenure predictor (`tenure_c`) from `Tenure_Months`.

**Model_5:** `Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c + Promo + tenure_c + emails_c*Promo + emails_c*tenure_c + Promo*tenure_c + emails_c*Promo*tenure_c`

```r
Model_5 <- lm(
  Monthly_Spend ~ emails_c + Promo + tenure_c +
    emails_c*Promo + emails_c*tenure_c + Promo*tenure_c +
    emails_c*Promo*tenure_c,
  data = exam2
)
summary(Model_5)
```

**Q43** (text entry) — Report the coefficient for the three-way interaction term `emails_c:Promo:tenure_c`.

**Q44** (MC) — Is the three-way interaction statistically significant at α = .05?

**Q45** (MC) — What is the conceptual meaning of a significant three-way interaction among emails, promo, and tenure?

**Q46** (text entry) — Using Model_5, report the predicted effect of each additional email for Customer A, defined as: Promo = 1, tenure_c = +1 SD above the mean.

**Q47** (text entry) — Using Model_5, report the predicted effect of each additional email for Customer B, defined as: Promo = 1, tenure_c = −1 SD below the mean.

**Q48** (MC) — Based on the slopes for Customers A and B, which customer is more responsive to email volume?

**Q49** (MC) — Which group is least appropriate for aggressive email and promo campaigns, given the interaction patterns you found?

**Q50** (MC) — How should the firm use tenure when designing email and promo strategies?

---

### Part 7 — One-Way ANOVA: Promotion Status and Tenure Groups (Questions 55–61)

#### Section A: Promotion as a categorical predictor

Create a factor version of the `Promo` variable and confirm it is coded as a factor before fitting models.

```r
exam2$PromoCategorical <- as.factor(exam2$Promo)
class(exam2$PromoCategorical)
levels(exam2$PromoCategorical)
```

**Model_6_compact:** `Monthly_Spend ~ 1`

**Model_6:** `Monthly_Spend ~ PromoCategorical`

```r
Model_6_compact <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ 1,                data = exam2)
Model_6         <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ PromoCategorical, data = exam2)
summary(Model_6)
anova(Model_6_compact, Model_6)
```

**Q51** (text entry) — From Model_6, report the PRE for `PromoCategorical` as a predictor of `Monthly_Spend`. Round to four decimal places.

**Q52** (MC) — Is the omnibus F test for Model_6 statistically significant at α = .05?

**Q53** (MC) — Is one-way ANOVA with a binary factor equivalent to simple regression with a 0/1 numeric predictor?

#### Section B: Tenure as a grouped categorical predictor

The firm's executive has asked that customer tenure be presented in terms of three groups: **Short** (fewer than 12 months), **Medium** (12 to 24 months inclusive), and **Long** (more than 24 months). Use `case_when()` from the `dplyr` package to create `Tenure_Group`, then confirm it is coded as a factor before fitting models.

```r
exam2 <- exam2 %>%
  mutate(
    Tenure_Group = case_when(
      Tenure_Months < 12  ~ "Short",
      Tenure_Months <= 24 ~ "Medium",
      Tenure_Months > 24  ~ "Long"
    )
  )

exam2$Tenure_Group <- as.factor(exam2$Tenure_Group)
class(exam2$Tenure_Group)
levels(exam2$Tenure_Group)
```

**Model_7_compact:** `Monthly_Spend ~ 1`

**Model_7:** `Monthly_Spend ~ Tenure_Group`

**Model_7_continuous:** `Monthly_Spend ~ tenure_c`

```r
Model_7_compact    <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ 1,            data = exam2)
Model_7            <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ Tenure_Group, data = exam2)
Model_7_continuous <- lm(Monthly_Spend ~ tenure_c,     data = exam2)

summary(Model_7)
anova(Model_7_compact, Model_7)
summary(Model_7_continuous)
```

**Q54** (text entry) — From Model_7, report the PRE for `Tenure_Group` as a predictor of `Monthly_Spend`. Round to four decimal places.

**Q55** (text entry) — From Model_7_continuous, report the PRE for `tenure_c` as a predictor of `Monthly_Spend`. Round to four decimal places.

**Q56** (MC) — Comparing the PRE from Model_7 (binned) to Model_7_continuous (continuous), what happened when tenure was converted to three groups?

**Q57** (MC) — The executive argues the grouped version is better because it is simpler. What is the most accurate analytical response?

---

### Part 8 — Code, Output, AI Disclosure, and Open Response (Questions 51–54)

Upload your modified R code file (the script with working code capable of reproducing your answers).

Upload the console output from RStudio corresponding to your exam analyses.

AI use disclosure, consistent with class policy (see syllabus).

Optional open response: space to share anything else before you conclude.

---

## AI Disclosure Reminder

Any use of AI tools must be disclosed in your submission consistent with class policy. See the syllabus and Q53 on the exam for full requirements. If AI was not used, simply write: *"AI used: No."*
